msgs.text: Is this a correct mathematical proof of the presentation for the absolute Galois group of the field of 2-adic numbers as a profinite group?

No. I would not accept the manuscript as a valid proof in its present form. 

A concrete error occurs in the proof of Lemma 6.5 on page 10. It asserts that every invariant quadratic polynomial on a regular module \(\mathbf F_2[H_V]^N\) is a sum of terms of the form
\[
\sum_{h\in H_V}X_{r,h}X_{s,hg}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\sum_{h\in H_V}X_{r,h}^{2}.
\]
This is false when \(g\) is an involution. For one regular summand, the orbit sum
\[
P_g=\sum_{\{h,hg\}}X_hX_{hg},
\]
where each unordered pair is included once, is \(H_V\)-invariant. But the manuscript’s proposed term is
\[
\sum_{h\in H_V}X_hX_{hg}=2P_g=0
\]
over \(\mathbf F_2\), since every monomial is counted twice. Thus its claimed spanning family omits genuine invariant quadratic polynomials. 

This is not merely an irrelevant edge case. Take
\[
H_V=S_3=C_3\rtimes C_2
\]
and its natural simple module \(V=\mathbf F_2^2\). This is a permissible ramified tame module. The invariant nonsingular form
\[
q(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2
\]
and the equivariant map
\[
p:\mathbf F_2[S_3]\longrightarrow V,\qquad e_h\longmapsto h(1,0),
\]
produce precisely such involution-orbit terms in \(q\circ p\). Consequently, the argument purporting to show
\[
Q_{\mathrm{loc}}\big|_{X_+}=0
\]
does not work.

That failure is decisive: Lemma 6.5 is used to prove the local determinant zero-count in Proposition 6.6, which is then used in equation (31) and in the global induction concluding equality of finite-quotient counts. 

There are also major unfilled steps elsewhere: the “Heisenberg calculation” yielding the operator in equation (13) is asserted rather than carried out, and the page-16 transition involving the “filtration-one edge,” descent through \(B/T_0\), shearing, and the decomposition \(Q(c)+\rho^*\delta\) lacks the required spectral-sequence and cochain-level justification. 

The initial checks—its tame quotient and maximal pro-\(2\) quotient—appear broadly consistent with known theory. Therefore this does **not** show that the proposed relation itself is necessarily false. It shows that the manuscript does not establish the theorem. This is especially significant because the published 2023 treatment notes that the known dyadic explicit result does not cover \(\mathbf Q_2\), and explicitly does not attempt to provide generators and relations in this case.
Ok, please let us know how to address the gaps that you pointed out. Think through this rivetingly, novelly, and describe the manuscript edits we should make

The repairs are not merely expository. Section 5 can be completed by calculation, but Lemma 6.5 needs a genuinely new local argument, and the passage to equation (31) needs a structural rewrite. While working through that rewrite, one additional issue becomes visible: when \(T_0\neq 0\), the \(M\)-lift torsor does **not** project onto all of \(H^1_\rho(V)\), so equation (31) cannot currently be used as written. 

## 1. Replace the proof of Lemma 6.5, rather than patching it

Delete the sentence on page 10 claiming that every invariant quadratic polynomial on
\[
W=\mathbf F_2[H_V]^N
\]
is a sum of the two displayed kinds of full orbit sums. The correct orbit decomposition has four kinds of terms. Writing coordinates as \(X_{r,h}\), a basis of invariant quadratic polynomials consists of:

\[
S_r=\sum_{h\in H_V}X_{r,h}^2;
\]

\[
C_{r,s}(g)=\sum_{h\in H_V}X_{r,h}X_{s,hg},
\qquad r<s;
\]

\[
C_r(g)=\sum_{h\in H_V}X_{r,h}X_{r,hg},
\qquad g^2\neq 1,
\]
with \(g\) taken modulo \(g\leftrightarrow g^{-1}\); and, crucially,

\[
E_r(j)=
\sum_{\{h,hj\}\in H_V/\langle j\rangle}
X_{r,h}X_{r,hj},
\qquad j^2=1,\ j\neq 1.
\]

The last expression is the missing half-orbit sum. It is exactly what occurs when an unordered pair of coordinates has a stabilizer of order two.

The manuscript should then state a separate **stabilizer-aware Shapiro lemma**. For Shapiro coordinates \(\alpha_r\in H^1(K,\mathbf F_2)\), the first three types evaluate as ordinary corestrictions:

\[
S_r\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{K/\mathbf Q_2}(\alpha_r\smile\alpha_r),
\]

\[
C_{r,s}(g)\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{K/\mathbf Q_2}
(\alpha_r\smile g\alpha_s),
\]

and similarly for \(C_r(g)\). These vanish on \(U_{e+1}\) by the Hilbert-symbol orthogonality already used in the manuscript.

The fourth type is different. If \(L_j=K^{\langle j\rangle}\), it evaluates as

\[
E_r(j)\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{L_j/\mathbf Q_2}
\bigl(N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{K/L_j}(\alpha_r)\bigr),
\]

where \(N^{\mathrm{Ev}}\colon H^1(K,\mathbf F_2)\to H^2(L_j,\mathbf F_2)\) is the index-two multiplicative transfer, or Evens norm. It is a quadratic operation; it is not an ordinary cup product over \(K\), and its vanishing does not follow from
\(\alpha_r\smile j\alpha_r=0\). Index-two induction formulas naturally contain precisely these Evens-norm terms. 

Thus equation (21) should be replaced by an equation of the form

\[
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\mathrm{loc}}(x)
={}&
\sum_{\text{free orbits}}
\operatorname{cor}_{K/\mathbf Q_2}
(\alpha_r\smile g\alpha_s)\\
&+
\sum_{\substack{r\\j^2=1,\ j\ne1}}
B(v_r,jv_r)\,
\operatorname{cor}_{L_j/\mathbf Q_2}
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{K/L_j}(\alpha_r),
\end{aligned}
\tag{21'}
\]

where \(v_r=p(e_{r,1})\), and the \(\alpha_r\) are the Shapiro coordinates of \(i_*x\).

The first line vanishes by the existing filtration argument. The entire burden is therefore concentrated in a new proposition:

> **Proposition 6.5A — involution cancellation.**  
> For \(x\in\operatorname{Hom}_{H_V}(V^\vee,U_{e+1})\), and for any split pair
> \[
> V\overset{i}{\longrightarrow}\mathbf F_2[H_V]^N
> \overset{p}{\longrightarrow}V,
> \qquad pi=1,
> \]
> the sum of the order-two orbit contributions in the second line of (21′) is zero.

This proposition must receive a complete cochain-level or local-arithmetic proof. It cannot be replaced by “the remaining terms are similar.”

A useful way to organize its proof is:

1. Prove the index-two formula for \(E_r(j)\) directly from a normalized Shapiro cochain map.
2. Observe that every order-two subgroup in a tame image corresponds to an unramified quadratic intermediate extension.
3. Express the total sum canonically, so that it is visibly independent of the chosen projective splitting \(i,p\).
4. Use the tame self-duality of \(V\), together with the relations among the coefficients \(B(v_r,jv_r)\), to prove cancellation of the Evens-norm terms.

The first mandatory test case is

\[
H_V=S_3,\qquad V=\mathbf F_2^2,\qquad
q(x,y)=x^2+xy+y^2.
\]

Here the half-orbit terms really occur. The appendix should calculate the unique nonzero element of \(X_+\) and verify its \(Q_8\)-embedding obstruction is zero. That computation is not a proof of the general proposition, but it is an essential regression test.

Until Proposition 6.5A is proved, Proposition 6.6 and the main theorem should be labelled conditional. The current page-10 argument does not establish them. 

## 2. Turn the “Heisenberg calculation” into an actual calculation

After Lemma 5.1, insert a new lemma giving the Fox–Heisenberg formula for an arbitrary finite word. It should define:

- the first derivative \(D_w\);
- the mixed derivative \(\beta_w\);
- the effect of inverse letters;
- the effect of a profinite power after replacing \(\omega_2\) by a suitable finite integer representative;
- the identity identifying \(\beta_w\) with the cellular Alexander–Whitney cup product.

Then add a table for the two simple-module cases.

Every class in \(H^1_\rho(V)\) has a representative

\[
z_c=(0,0,c,0)
\]

on \((\sigma,\tau,x_0,x_1)\), and similarly \(z_\lambda\) for \(V^\vee\). The table should read:

\[
\begin{array}{c|c|c}
\text{contribution}
&T=1&V^T=0\\ \hline
r_{\mathrm t}&0&0\\
x_0^2&\lambda(c)&\lambda(c)\\
[d_0,z_0]&0&
\lambda(Uc)+\lambda(U^{-1}c)\\
\text{other factors of }r_{\mathrm w}&0&0.
\end{array}
\]

Indeed, in the ramified case the first derivatives are

\[
D d_0(c)=c,\qquad D z_0(c)=U^{-1}c,
\]

while on the dual side

\[
D d_0(\lambda)=\lambda,\qquad
D z_0(\lambda)=U^\vee\lambda.
\]

The mixed central coordinate of the commutator is therefore

\[
\lambda(U^{-1}c)+(U^\vee\lambda)(c)
=
\lambda\bigl((U^{-1}+U)c\bigr).
\]

Adding the contribution from \(x_0^2\) gives

\[
\lambda\bigl((1+U+U^{-1})c\bigr),
\]

which proves equation (13). In the unramified case the commutator contributes zero, leaving \(\lambda(c)\).

This should appear in the body of the proof, not behind the phrase “the Heisenberg calculation identifies.” The full general word formula can go in an appendix, but the four-line computation above belongs immediately before equation (13).

Also add a short finite-exponent lemma: if two integers represent \(\omega_2\) modulo the exponent of every semidirect and Heisenberg group being used, then both the first and mixed derivatives coincide. That closes the present gap between the profinite word and the finite-word calculation.

## 3. Make Lemmas 6.8 and 6.9 cochain-level statements

The phrase “a central class has LHS components \(\delta+\gamma+q\)” is currently too informal. Components in the Lyndon–Hochschild–Serre spectral sequence are associated-graded data, not automatically a canonical direct-sum decomposition.

For Lemma 6.8, include the relevant part of the seven-term sequence or an explicit bicomplex calculation, and prove

\[
d_2(q)=B_q^\flat{}_*(\eta).
\]

Specify that “\(q\) survives” means that it is the restriction to \(V\) of an actual class in \(H^2(B,\mathbf F_2)\). Then \(d_2(q)=0\), and nonsingularity of \(B_q\) indeed implies \(\eta=0\).

For Lemma 6.9, after choosing a splitting \(B=V\rtimes C\) and a normalized cocycle \(f_q\) representing the determinant extension of \(V\), write an actual representative:

\[
\kappa((v,c),(w,d))
=
f_q(v,cw)+\gamma(c)(cw)+\delta(c,d).
\tag{29A}
\]

Then compute the pullback under the shear

\[
s_a(v,c)=(v+a(c),c).
\]

The calculation should show explicitly that

\[
\gamma\longmapsto\gamma+B_q^\flat(a),
\]

while the remaining change is an inflated \(2\)-cocycle on \(C\). Since \(B_q^\flat\) is an isomorphism, one chooses \(a\) to kill \(\gamma\). This gives a genuine proof of the normal form

\[
\kappa\sim q+\operatorname{inf}(\delta').
\]

The manuscript should also verify in this calculation—not merely assert—that the shear preserves the marked subgroup and scalar decorations.

## 4. Replace equation (31) by a constrained quadratic count

This is the most important global edit.

The manuscript currently moves from \(M\) to its simple head \(V\), then counts all values \(c\in H^1_\rho(V)\) using \(N_0(Q)\) and \(N_1(Q)\). But the coefficient sequence

\[
0\longrightarrow T_0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow0
\]

gives a long exact sequence of deformation cohomology:

\[
H^1_\rho(M)
\longrightarrow H^1_\rho(V)
\overset{\partial_\rho}{\longrightarrow}
H^2_\rho(T_0)
\longrightarrow H^2_\rho(M).
\tag{30A}
\]

The manuscript has already proved \(H^2_\rho(M)=0\). Hence \(\partial_\rho\) is surjective.

Moreover, if \(T_0\neq0\), then \(T_0^\vee\) has a nonzero \(C\)-fixed vector because all its composition factors are trivial. Perfect deformation duality therefore gives

\[
H^2_\rho(T_0)\neq0.
\]

Consequently, the image of \(H^1_\rho(M)\) in \(H^1_\rho(V)\) is the proper subspace

\[
\ker\partial_\rho.
\]

Thus equation (31), which inserts the zero counts of \(Q\) over the whole of \(H^1_\rho(V)\), is justified only when \(T_0=0\). The page-16 argument does not currently account for this restriction. 

The correct object is an affine subspace. For every lower map \(\rho\), define

\[
\mathcal A_{\Gamma,\rho}
=
\left\{
c\in H^1_\rho(V):
\partial_\rho(c)=\eta_{\Gamma,\rho}
\right\},
\]

where \(\eta_{\Gamma,\rho}\) is the affine lifting defect of the particular extension \(B\to B/T_0\). The replacement for equation (31) should be

\[
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
\sum_{\rho\in X_\Gamma(C)}
\mu_{\Gamma,\rho}\,
\#\left\{
c\in\mathcal A_{\Gamma,\rho}:
Q_\Gamma(c)+\rho^*\delta=0
\right\}.
\tag{31'}
\]

A separate lemma should prove that the fiber multiplicity \(\mu_{\Gamma,\rho}\) is independent of \(\rho\) and equal on the two source groups.

### The clean way to evaluate (31′)

Use a joint Fourier–Gauss transform.

Put

\[
W=H^1_\rho(V),\qquad
D=H^2_\rho(T_0).
\]

For \(b\in\mathbf F_2\), the number of \(c\in W\) satisfying

\[
\partial_\rho(c)=\eta,\qquad Q(c)=b
\]

equals

\[
\frac{1}{2|D|}
\sum_{\chi\in D^\vee}
(-1)^{\chi(\eta)}
\left[
\sum_{c\in W}(-1)^{\chi(\partial_\rho c)}
+
(-1)^b
\sum_{c\in W}
(-1)^{Q(c)+\chi(\partial_\rho c)}
\right].
\tag{31A}
\]

For each \(\chi\), let

\[
\ell_\chi=\chi\circ\partial_\rho.
\]

Since the polar form \(B_Q\) is nonsingular, there is a unique \(v_\chi\in W\) such that

\[
\ell_\chi(c)=B_Q(v_\chi,c).
\]

The quadratic Gauss identity gives

\[
\sum_{c\in W}(-1)^{Q(c)+\ell_\chi(c)}
=
(-1)^{Q(v_\chi)+\operatorname{Arf}(Q)}
\,2^{\dim W/2}.
\tag{31B}
\]

This is the robust replacement for the manuscript’s “either one half survive, or the class descends” dichotomy. It handles nonzero edges, zero edges, nontrivial \(T_0\), and affine shifts uniformly.

To close the induction, insert one further proposition:

> **Phase-class proposition.**  
> For every \(\chi\in D^\vee\), there is a class
> \[
> \Delta_\chi\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2),
> \]
> depending only on the target extensions and \(\chi\), such that
> \[
> Q_\Gamma(v_{\chi,\rho})
> +
> \chi(\eta_{\Gamma,\rho})
> =
> \rho^*\Delta_\chi.
> \tag{31C}
> \]

Once (31C) is proved, the phases in (31A) are ordinary scalar central-cover obstructions. They can be compared by the same exact-image transform already used for \(\delta\) in equation (32). This makes the induction genuinely closed.

When \(T_0=0\), \(D=0\), formula (31A) collapses to the manuscript’s present equation (31). Thus the current argument should be retained only as the terminal \(T_0=0\) subcase.

## 5. Recommended manuscript reorganization

A sound revised structure would be:

1. **Section 5.2:** state the Fox–Heisenberg lemma.
2. **New Section 5.3:** give the explicit table proving equation (13).
3. **Section 6.3:** replace Lemma 6.5 by:
   - invariant orbit decomposition;
   - order-two/Evens-norm evaluation;
   - involution-cancellation proposition.
4. **Section 6.4:** replace informal spectral-sequence components by explicit seven-term and cocycle-normal-form lemmas.
5. **New Section 8.3:** prove the constrained Fourier–Gauss identity (31A)–(31B).
6. **Section 9.3:** use the affine spaces \(\mathcal A_{\Gamma,\rho}\), prove the phase-class proposition, and then invoke central-cover induction.
7. **Appendix:** include the \(S_3\) calculation and machine checks of the Fox derivatives.

Until the involution-cancellation and phase-class propositions are proved, the abstract should say that the proposed presentation is *reduced to two explicit quadratic identities*, rather than “We prove.” Once those two propositions are established, the rest of the proof can be made auditable and the main theorem restored without qualification.

Please bring in existing ideas and novel ideas to prove the remaining statements: <Until the involution-cancellation and phase-class propositions are proved,> Use the research turns, if helpful ( attached)

The two gaps can be closed, but the clean proofs differ from the tentative propositions I suggested earlier:

1. The involution terms do not need to cancel against one another; each missing Evens-norm term vanishes individually on the deep-unit subspace.
2. The phase class is the inflated component produced by shearing a universal mixed determinant class. Combining that construction with Fourier–Gauss inversion and the central-cover transform gives the correct replacement for equation (31).

The attached research log already contains the essential involution calculation and the central-cover counting mechanism. 

## 1. Involution-cancellation: replace it by termwise Evens-norm vanishing

The assertion presently used in Lemma 6.5—that every invariant quadratic polynomial on a regular module is a sum of full orbit sums—is false. The complete orbit classification is as follows.

Let
\[
W=\mathbf F_2[H]^N
\]
with coordinates \(X_{r,h}\), and let \(H\) act by left translation on \(h\). Every orbit of a degree-two monomial is of exactly one of these types:

\[
S_r=\sum_{h\in H}X_{r,h}^2;
\]

\[
C_{r,s,g}=\sum_{h\in H}X_{r,h}X_{s,hg},
\]
when the unordered monomial has trivial stabilizer; and

\[
E_{r,g}
=
\sum_{h\in H/\langle g\rangle}
X_{r,h}X_{r,hg},
\qquad g^2=1,\quad g\neq1.
\tag{IC1}
\]

Indeed, an element stabilizing
\[
\{(r,1),(s,g)\}
\]
must either fix both entries, hence be the identity, or exchange them. Exchange requires \(r=s\) and \(g^2=1\). Thus the involution half-orbits are the only omitted case.

Let \(K/F\) be the tame splitting field with group \(H\), and let
\[
\alpha_r\in H^1(K,\mathbf F_2)
\]
be the scalar Shapiro coordinates. The corresponding quadratic operations are

\[
S_r\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{K/F}(\alpha_r^2),
\]

\[
C_{r,s,g}\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{K/F}(\alpha_r\smile g\alpha_s),
\]

and

\[
E_{r,g}\longmapsto
\operatorname{cor}_{K_0/F}
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{K/K_0}(\alpha_r),
\qquad
K_0=K^{\langle g\rangle}.
\tag{IC2}
\]

The last operation is the degree-one index-two Evens norm. It arises by first applying quadratic Shapiro to the two-point regular \(\langle g\rangle\)-orbit: the invariant polynomial \(XY\) is precisely the Evens norm. Corestriction from \(K_0\) then sums over \(H/\langle g\rangle\). This is the orbit–stabilizer Shapiro formula reached in Turn 114. 

Because tame inertia has odd order, \(\langle g\rangle\) meets inertia trivially. Therefore \(K/K_0\) is an unramified quadratic extension.

### Deep-unit Evens-norm theorem

The required local statement is:

> Let \(L/k\) be an unramified quadratic extension of finite dyadic local fields, and put \(e=v_k(2)\). Then
> \[
> N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}(\alpha)=0
> \quad\text{for every}\quad
> \alpha\in U_{e+1}(L)\subset L^\times/L^{\times2}.
> \tag{IC3}
> \]

Here is a direct proof.

Write
\[
L=k(\sqrt d),\qquad
a=u+v\sqrt d,\qquad
n=N_{L/k}(a)=u^2-dv^2.
\]
The index-two Stiefel–Whitney induction formula, together with
\[
\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}\langle a\rangle
\simeq
\left\langle 2u,\frac{2dn}{u}\right\rangle,
\qquad
\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}\langle1\rangle
\simeq
\langle2,2d\rangle,
\]
gives, in additive Hilbert-symbol notation,
\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}([a])
=
(u,-d)+(2du,n).
\tag{IC4}
\]

Now assume \(a\in U_{e+1}(L)\). Since
\[
U_{e+1}(L)=1+2\mathfrak p_L,
\]
write \(a=1+2b\), with \(b\in\mathfrak p_L\). Put
\[
t=\operatorname{Tr}_{L/k}(b),
\qquad
m=N_{L/k}(b).
\]
Then
\[
u=1+t,\qquad
n=1+2t+4m.
\]
Since \(v_k(m)\ge2\),
\[
\frac{n}{2u-1}
=
1+\frac{4m}{2u-1}
\in U_{2e+2}(k)\subset k^{\times2}.
\tag{IC5}
\]
This is the correct orientation of the ratio; the displayed version of (114.8) in the research log should be typeset this way.

Every unit of \(k\) is a norm from the unramified quadratic extension, so
\[
(u,d)=0.
\]
Also \(n=N_{L/k}(a)\), hence
\[
(d,n)=0.
\]
Consequently (IC4) becomes
\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{L/k}([a])
=
(u,-1)+(2u,2u-1).
\]
The Steinberg relation applied to \(2u\) gives
\[
(2u,2u-1)=(2u,-1).
\]
Finally,
\[
(2,-1)=0
\]
because \(2=N_{k(i)/k}(1+i)\). Therefore
\[
(2u,-1)=(u,-1),
\]
and the two terms cancel. This proves (IC3). 

### Consequence for Lemma 6.5

For a deep Shapiro class, every \(\alpha_r\) lies in \(U_{e+1}(K)\). Therefore:

\[
\alpha_r\smile g\alpha_s=0
\]
for the free product orbits by Hilbert orthogonality;

\[
\alpha_r^2
=
\alpha_r\smile(-1)=0
\]
for square orbits; and

\[
N^{\mathrm{Ev}}_{K/K_0}(\alpha_r)=0
\]
for the involution-stabilized orbits.

Hence every orbit contribution vanishes separately:
\[
Q_{\mathrm{loc}}\big|_{X_+}=0.
\]

So the proposed “involution-cancellation proposition” should be replaced by the stronger and simpler termwise-vanishing theorem above. No identities among coefficients \(B(v_r,gv_r)\) are needed.

## 2. The phase-class proposition

The current equation (31) still requires a correction. When
\[
0\longrightarrow T_0\longrightarrow M\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow0,
\]
the long exact sequence of deformation cohomology contains
\[
H^1_\rho(M)\longrightarrow H^1_\rho(V)
\xrightarrow{\partial_\rho}
H^2_\rho(T_0)
\longrightarrow H^2_\rho(M).
\]
Since \(H^2_\rho(M)=0\), the map \(\partial_\rho\) is surjective. Thus the \(V\)-coordinates of actual \(M\)-lifts occupy an affine fibre
\[
\mathcal A_\rho
=
\{c\in H^1_\rho(V):\partial_\rho c=\eta_\rho\},
\tag{PC1}
\]
not all of \(H^1_\rho(V)\). Therefore the unrestricted \(N_0(Q),N_1(Q)\) formula on page 16 is justified only when \(T_0=0\). 

The missing phase class can, however, be constructed canonically.

### Construction

Write
\[
W_\rho=H^1_\rho(V),
\qquad
D_\rho=H^2_\rho(T_0).
\]
Perfect deformation duality identifies
\[
D_\rho^\vee\simeq (T_0^\vee)^C.
\]

Fix
\[
\chi\in(T_0^\vee)^C.
\]
Dualize the module sequence:
\[
0\longrightarrow V^\vee
\longrightarrow M^\vee
\longrightarrow T_0^\vee
\longrightarrow0.
\]
Let
\[
\gamma_\chi\in H^1(C,V^\vee)
\tag{PC2}
\]
be the image of \(\chi\) under its connecting homomorphism. Equivalently, \(\gamma_\chi\) is the class of the module extension obtained by pushing
\[
0\to T_0\to M\to V\to0
\]
out along \(\chi:T_0\to\mathbf F_2\).

Let
\[
b_q^\flat:V\xrightarrow{\sim}V^\vee
\]
be the isomorphism induced by the polar form of the nonsingular determinant form \(q\). Define
\[
a_\chi=(b_q^\flat)^{-1}_*(\gamma_\chi)
\in H^1(C,V).
\tag{PC3}
\]

Naturality of the deformation duality with respect to the short exact sequence gives the adjointness identity
\[
\chi(\partial_\rho c)
=
\langle c,\rho^*\gamma_\chi\rangle_\rho
=
B_{Q,\rho}\bigl(c,\rho^*a_\chi\bigr).
\tag{PC4}
\]
Thus the unique vector representing the linear functional
\(\chi\circ\partial_\rho\) under the polar form is
\[
v_{\chi,\rho}=\rho^*a_\chi.
\tag{PC5}
\]

After the determinant transgression has forced
\[
B/T_0\simeq V\rtimes C,
\]
choose a splitting. Let
\[
e\in H^2(C,T_0)
\]
be the residual \(T_0\)-extension class along the zero section. Then
\[
\eta_\rho=\rho^*e.
\tag{PC6}
\]

Let
\[
\Theta_q(a_\chi)\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2)
\]
be the obstruction obtained by pulling the extraspecial extension defined by \(q\) back along the graph cocycle \(a_\chi\). Define
\[
\boxed{
\Delta_\chi
=
\chi_*e+\Theta_q(a_\chi)
\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2).
}
\tag{PC7}
\]

Naturality of the quadratic connecting operation gives
\[
Q_\rho(\rho^*a_\chi)
=
\rho^*\Theta_q(a_\chi),
\]
while (PC6) gives
\[
\chi(\eta_\rho)=\rho^*(\chi_*e).
\]
Therefore
\[
\boxed{
Q_\rho(v_{\chi,\rho})+\chi(\eta_\rho)
=
\rho^*\Delta_\chi.
}
\tag{PC8}
\]

This is exactly the phase-class proposition.

Conceptually, \(\Delta_\chi\) is the inflated component obtained by applying the manuscript’s shear to the mixed LHS class
\[
\chi_*e+\gamma_\chi+q.
\]
The shear by \(a_\chi\) kills \(\gamma_\chi\) and changes the inflated component by \(\Theta_q(a_\chi)\). Thus (PC7) is also independent of the cochain presentation of the construction.

## 3. Correct replacement for equation (31)

Let
\[
n=\dim W_\rho,
\qquad
b_\rho=\rho^*\delta\in\mathbf F_2.
\]
We must count
\[
N_\rho
=
\#\{c\in W_\rho:
\partial_\rho c=\eta_\rho,\;
Q_\rho(c)+b_\rho=0\}.
\]

Character orthogonality and the quadratic Gauss sum give
\[
N_\rho
=
\frac{1}{2|D_\rho|}
\left[
2^n+
(-1)^{\operatorname{Arf}(Q_\rho)}
2^{n/2}
\sum_{\chi\in D_\rho^\vee}
(-1)^{b_\rho+Q_\rho(v_{\chi,\rho})+\chi(\eta_\rho)}
\right].
\tag{PC9}
\]
Here surjectivity of \(\partial_\rho\) makes the nonquadratic character sum vanish for every \(\chi\neq0\).

Using (PC8),
\[
N_\rho
=
\frac{1}{2|D_\rho|}
\left[
2^n+
(-1)^{\operatorname{Arf}(Q_\rho)}
2^{n/2}
\sum_{\chi\in D_\rho^\vee}
(-1)^{\rho^*(\delta+\Delta_\chi)}
\right].
\tag{PC10}
\]

Now sum over the lower exact-image maps
\[
X_\Gamma(C).
\]
For a scalar class \(\kappa\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2)\), put
\[
s_\Gamma(\kappa)
=
\sum_{\rho\in X_\Gamma(C)}
(-1)^{\rho^*\kappa}.
\]
Then
\[
s_\Gamma(\kappa)
=
2n_{\Gamma,0}(\kappa)-|X_\Gamma(C)|,
\]
where
\[
n_{\Gamma,0}(\kappa)
=
\#\{\rho\in X_\Gamma(C):\rho^*\kappa=0\}.
\]

The correct replacement for equation (31) is therefore
\[
\boxed{
Z_{\Gamma,\lambda}(B/C)
=
\frac{\mu}{2|D|}
\left[
2^n|X_\Gamma(C)|
+
(-1)^{\operatorname{Arf}(Q)}
2^{n/2}
\sum_{\chi\in D^\vee}
s_\Gamma(\delta+\Delta_\chi)
\right].
}
\tag{31*}
\]

When \(T_0=0\), \(D=0\), and (31*) reduces exactly to the manuscript’s present formula. Thus the old formula is recovered as the terminal special case.

For every \(\chi\), the zero count
\[
n_{\Gamma,0}(\delta+\Delta_\chi)
\]
is an ordinary central-cover liftability count for the central extension
\[
C_{\delta+\Delta_\chi}\longrightarrow C.
\]
The exact identity from the research turns is
\[
8\,n_{\Gamma,0}(\kappa)
=
\sum_{\substack{\widetilde J\le C_\kappa\\
p_\kappa(\widetilde J)=C}}
e_\Gamma(\widetilde J,\ldots).
\tag{PC11}
\]
It is a literal partition of the eight scalar lifts by exact image, not an equidistribution assertion. 

The same strict-descent estimate as the manuscript’s equation (33) applies:
\[
|\widetilde J\cap\widetilde L|
\le
2|L_C|
=
\frac{2|L_Y|}{|K|}
<
|L_Y|,
\]
since the nontrivial simple head has dimension at least two and hence \(|K|\ge4\). Thus every phase class is controlled by the global induction without circularity.

## 4. Concrete manuscript edits

On page 10, replace the claimed full-orbit spanning statement in Lemma 6.5 by:

1. **Quadratic orbit–stabilizer Shapiro lemma**, with the three orbit types (IC1) and images (IC2).
2. **Deep-unit Evens-norm lemma**, with proof (IC3)–(IC5).
3. The three-line conclusion that squares, free products, and involution orbits all vanish on \(X_+\).

On page 16:

1. Insert the long exact sequence and explicitly define \(\partial_\rho\) and \(\eta_\rho\).
2. State and prove the phase-descent proposition (PC2)–(PC8).
3. Delete the present equation (31).
4. Insert (PC9), (PC10), and the aggregated formula (31*).
5. Replace the single auxiliary cover \(C_\delta\) in equation (32) by the family
   \[
   C_{\delta+\Delta_\chi},
   \qquad \chi\in(T_0^\vee)^C.
   \]
6. Retain the existing strict marked-kernel bound.

These changes close both named gaps without altering the proposed defining relation. They do not by themselves constitute an independent end-to-end certification of every other lemma in the manuscript, but the involution and affine-phase obstructions are no longer unresolved.
